Chart NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) earnings surprises over the last 8 quarters — plot actual EPS vs estimated EPS as two lines over the 8 report dates, with the surprise delta (actualEarningResult minus epsEstimated) as bars on a secondary axis below, and color each quarter's bar green for beat / red for miss so I can read NVDA's beat streak at a glance.

Compare the most recent quarterly EPS surprise delta across AAPL, MSFT, NVDA, AMZN, GOOGL, META, and TSLA — rank all 7 mega-caps from biggest beat to biggest miss as a horizontal bar chart, with the surprise delta value ($) labeled on each bar and the revenue surprise % shown as a second overlaid series so I can see which giants are surprising on margin vs on the top line.

Plot Eli Lilly and Company's (LLY) quarterly revenue actual vs estimated and EPS actual vs estimated over the last 8 quarters as dual lines on two panels, and chart the revenue surprise % next to the EPS surprise % per quarter so I can see whether LLY's beats are driven by revenue strength or by margin/expansion.

Aggregate Exxon Mobil Corporation's (XOM) insider Form 4 transactions over the last 90 days by week and chart the net dollar insider flow (sum of P-Purchase value minus sum of S-Sale value) per week as a bar chart over the 13 weeks, labeling which weeks insiders were net buyers (green) vs net sellers (red) and the cumulative net flow as a running line.

Pull Meta Platforms' (META) US congressional trades over the last 120 days and chart them by politician — one grouped bar per politician showing reported buy amount vs sell amount side by side — so I can see at a glance which House representatives and senators are most active in META and on which side of the trade.

Rank AAPL, MSFT, NVDA, AMZN, GOOGL, META, and TSLA by DCF upside % — (dcfPrice minus stockPrice) divided by stockPrice — as a horizontal bar chart from cheapest (most upside) to most expensive (most downside), with each ticker's EV/EBITDA multiple labeled on its bar so I can see which mega-caps look intrinsically undervalued versus which trade rich on enterprise multiples.

Plot Tesla, Inc.'s (TSLA) multi-period price changes (1W, 1M, 3M, 6M, YTD, 1Y) as bars alongside TSLA's EV/EBITDA and EV/Sales multiples as two lines on a secondary axis, so I can see whether TSLA's recent returns are supported by cheaper or richer enterprise multiples across each lookback window.

Pull JPMorgan Chase & Co.'s (JPM) FMP peer group and rank JPM and each peer from cheapest to richest on DCF upside vs current price, with EV/EBITDA and the latest annual revenue growth % shown as side columns, then render the group as a bar chart sorted by DCF upside so I can read the relative-value table and the ranking chart together.

Rank all 11 GICS sectors (Technology, Healthcare, Financial Services, Energy, Consumer Cyclical, Consumer Defensive, Communication Services, Industrials, Utilities, Real Estate, Basic Materials) by YTD return as a bar chart, with each sector's average P/E overlaid as a line on a secondary axis, so I can see which sectors are leading on YTD return and whether their valuations look stretched or cheap relative to that performance.

Within the Technology sector, chart YTD performance across its industries (Semiconductors, Software - Infrastructure, Software - Application, Communication Equipment, Electronic Components, IT Services, Computer Hardware, Scientific & Technical Instruments, etc.) as a ranked bar chart from best to worst YTD return, labeling each industry's YTD % so I can see which Technology industries are leading the sector's rotation.

Plot 5-day and 1-month returns for all 11 GICS sectors (Technology, Healthcare, Financial Services, Energy, Consumer Cyclical, Consumer Defensive, Communication Services, Industrials, Utilities, Real Estate, Basic Materials) as a grouped bar chart with two bars per sector, sorted by 1-month return, so I can see which sectors have short-term 5-day momentum building or fading relative to their 1-month trend.

Chart Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) most recent fiscal-year revenue split by geography (Americas, Europe, Greater China, Japan, Rest of Asia Pacific) as a grouped bar chart with the prior-year revenue per region shown as a second bar per region, and label the YoY % change on each region so I can see which geography is growing and which is shrinking.

Plot Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) revenue by product line (iPhone, Mac, iPad, Wearables/Home/Accessories, Services) over the last 8 fiscal quarters as a multi-line chart, one line per product line, so I can see which product line is driving AAPL's growth and which is decelerating over the 8 quarters.

Chart Amazon.com, Inc.'s (AMZN) annual growth rates for revenue, EBITDA, free cash flow, and EPS over the last 8 fiscal years as a multi-line chart, and call out in the narrative whether AMZN's growth is accelerating or decelerating across each of the 4 metrics year over year.

Compare free float % and shares outstanding for AAPL, MSFT, NVDA, AMZN, GOOGL, META, and TSLA as a grouped bar chart, then overlay each ticker's Altman Z-score and DCF upside % as two lines on a secondary axis, so I can see whether the most widely-held mega-caps also score well on quality and intrinsic value. Note explicitly if any 13F institutional holder data is unavailable at the current FMP tier rather than guessing.

Build a one-pass investment-thesis view on JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM): pull JPM's free float %, shares outstanding, Altman Z / Piotroski F / Beneish M scores, and DCF fair value vs current price, then chart the three quality scores (Z, F, M) as bars with the DCF upside % labeled alongside, so I can read whether JPM's float structure is supported by quality and intrinsic value. State explicitly if the 13F institutional holder table is unavailable at the current FMP tier.

Plot the current US Treasury yield curve across all maturities (1M, 3M, 6M, 1Y, 2Y, 5Y, 10Y, 20Y, 30Y) as a line chart with each maturity's yield % labeled, and explicitly call out whether the curve is normal, flat, or inverted between the 2Y and 10Y maturities using the 2Y-10Y spread in basis points.

Over the next 14 days, chart every major US macro event (FOMC, CPI, Employment Situation, Retail Sales, PPI, ISM Manufacturing PMI, Consumer Confidence) on a date timeline as bars with each event's importance rating (High/Medium/Low) encoded as the bar height, so I can see which weeks are macro-heavy and which are quiet leading into the next FOMC and CPI prints.

Compare Altman Z-score, Piotroski F-score, and Beneish M-score across AAPL, MSFT, NVDA, AMZN, GOOGL, META, and TSLA as a grouped bar chart with three bars per ticker (one per score), so I can see at a glance which mega-caps score highest on bankruptcy-distance quality and which carry the highest Beneish manipulation signal.

For Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), chart the Altman Z, Piotroski F, and Beneish M scores as bars against their distress thresholds (Altman Z safe >3.0 / distress <1.8; Piotroski F high >7 / low <5; Beneish M safe >-1.78 / manipulator <-2.22) shown as horizontal reference lines, and label LLY's FMP rating grade alongside, so I can read at a glance whether LLY sits in the quality zone or the distress zone on each score.

Plot Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) quarterly dividend per share over the last 5 years (~20 quarters) as a step chart, labeling each ex-dividend date and the dividend amount on the step, so I can see JNJ's dividend growth trajectory, cadence, and any hike dates over the 5-year window.

Count Costco Wholesale Corporation's (COST) SEC filings over the last 120 days by formType (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, DEF 14A, 424B4, SC 13D) and chart the count per form type as a bar chart, then list the filing dates of any 8-K material events and 10-K annual reports underneath the chart so I can see COST's recent disclosure mix and the material-event dates in one view.

List every S&P 500 (^GSPC) constituent classified in the Semiconductors sub-industry and chart them by market cap as a horizontal bar chart sorted largest to smallest, with each ticker's sector and sub-industry labeled, so I can see the universe of large-cap semiconductor names in the S&P 500 at a glance.

Pull the Dow Jones Industrial Average (^DJI) 30 constituents and chart them grouped by GICS sector as a bar chart of constituent count per sector, labeling each ticker inside its sector group, so I can see the sector composition and concentration of the Dow 30 at a glance.

Plot Exxon Mobil Corporation's (XOM) daily closing price over the last 12 months (~252 trading days) as a line chart with the 50-day and 200-day simple moving averages overlaid as two additional lines, so I can see XOM's price trend over the year and whether XOM is currently trading above or below its key 50-day and 200-day moving averages.
